pinboard.title: Laws of Software Engineering
pinboard.description:
This is a handy catalog, but the deeper value is knowing when each one clarifies reality and when it distorts it. Brooks’s Law, Conway’s Law, Hyrum’s Law, Goodhart’s Law, Gall’s Law, YAGNI, DRY, KISS: each names a recurring pressure, tradeoff, or failure mode. The mistake is treating them as universal commandments instead of heuristics with scope and context. The best engineers do not cite laws to end discussion. They use them to ask better questions about systems, teams, and unintended consequences. 


HN thoughts: The thread is broadly skeptical of the framing. The strongest consensus is that calling these ideas “laws” oversells them: commenters repeatedly argue they are really aphorisms, heuristics, or design principles, often contradictory and too easy to cherry-pick to justify whatever decision someone already wants to make. 

A major subtheme is that the list is incomplete or misleading. People suggest missing entries like Greenspun’s rule, Boyd’s law of iteration, and Ousterhout’s ideas on complexity, while others say some listed items are not really software-engineering-specific at all.

The main disagreement is not whether these observations are useful, but how they should be used. One camp says many are valuable if treated as context-dependent guidance rather than doctrine. Examples include nuanced defenses of DRY, “premature optimization,” and decomposition principles. The other camp sees the whole exercise as vague software voodoo dressed up as science, with little empirical grounding.

There is also a smaller practical thread about books and sources worth trusting, plus one modern angle: using Claude or other LLMs to brute-force performance ideas, then porting the good parts back into maintainable code. Overall mood: useful maxims exist, but dogmatism and false precision are the real danger.
pinboard.byline_hint: Pinboard Popular · tagged: softwareengineering, management, architecture, decisionmaking, heuristics, systems-thinking

pinboard.title: The Mystery in the Medicine Cabinet—Asterisk
pinboard.description:
"How does acetaminophen work? Nobody knows!"

"When ibuprofen inhibits the COX enzymes, it does so throughout the body. And mostly, that is bad."

"If you take a lot of ibuprofen, this could hurt your liver. But the major concerns seem to be the stomach, the heart, and the kidneys."

"If you take too much acetaminophen at once, the pathways that break it down into harmless stuff get saturated, but the P450 system doesn’t get saturated. This means that not only is there more acetaminophen, but also that a much larger fraction of it is broken down into NAPQI."

"you should never take more than the recommended dose of  acetaminophen."

pinboard.title: What The Fuck Is An Elder Queer - Chapter 1 - luulapants - Heated Rivalry (TV) [Archive of Our Own]
pinboard.description:
Rozanov made a dismissive noise. “No, no, I will pick you up. Send me your address.”

“I’m not giving you my home address.”

“Why not!”

“Because you’ll probably post it on social media to psych me out before our next game.”

That made Rozanov laugh. “It is good idea,” he said. Stopped laughing. Started again. Blew out a breath like he had to get himself together. “I might do this to you,” he allowed, “but you have nice boyfriend living with you now. I would not do this to him. You are safe.”

 

In which Ilya Rozanov adopts himself into Scott and Kip's household and refuses to leave. Kip finds this very sweet. Scott is going to kill him.
